at_a_glance: Iran talks falter as pressure mounts; EU airlines warn of fuel shortages if the Strait of Hormuz stays closed and shipowners are urged not to pay Iranian tolls.
Ukraine and Russia trade accusations of hundreds of ceasefire violations amid persistent fighting.
At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site as authorities investigate safety failures.
British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer', intensifying debate over the UK's industrial strategy.
Fears grow for Argentina's glaciers, with warnings that every drop of water counts for future supplies.
Asha Bhosle, the iconic voice of Bollywood, dies aged 92.

stories.title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
stories.summary: Fal­tering talks have left the Gulf on edge as policymakers weigh how to prevent a wider crisis. The aviation and shipping sectors warn of knock‑on effects if disruption around the Strait of Hormuz persists, while political leaders urge renewed diplomacy.
body: With efforts to calm tensions with Iran stalling, attention is shifting to the immediate risks and the narrow diplomatic path available to avoid escalation. The stakes are high, not only for Washington and Tehran, but for regional capitals and global markets exposed to any miscalculation.

The Strait of Hormuz remains the critical pressure point. The EU airline industry has warned of potential jet fuel shortages if the waterway stays closed or restricted, and carriers are already drawing up contingency plans to manage supply and routing risks. Refiners and traders are assessing alternative flows, conscious that even short‑lived disruption can ripple across supply chains.

On the water, shipowners and charterers have been urged not to pay any Iranian tolls for passage, underscoring a fraught legal and commercial landscape. Insurers are scrutinising cover, war‑risk premiums are in focus, and energy markets are volatile as participants game out scenarios from prolonged disruption to a fragile reopening.

Seeking to steady nerves, political leaders have called for restraint and a route back to talks. Labour leader Keir Starmer urged the United States and Iran to find a way through, echoing wider appeals for de‑escalation. For now, back‑channel contacts and shuttle diplomacy appear the most viable tools to lower the temperature and reopen space for substantive dialogue.

stories.title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
stories.summary: Kyiv and Moscow have each reported hundreds of violations, reflecting a grinding conflict in which civilians and infrastructure remain at risk. Independent verification is scarce, and neither side shows signs of easing pressure along the front.
body: Both Ukraine and Russia accused the other of breaching ceasefire understandings hundreds of times, underscoring the persistent volatility of the front. The claims point to sustained exchanges of artillery, drones and small‑unit engagements as each side probes for openings.

Civilian areas near the line of contact continue to face danger, with damage to homes and utilities reported in multiple districts. Access for humanitarian teams remains uneven, complicating efforts to deliver aid and assess needs as winter gives way to a muddy spring.

Militarily, the pattern suggests a contest of attrition, with logistics, air defence and electronic warfare playing decisive roles. Rotations and re‑supply are under constant strain, and commanders appear intent on testing one another’s defences rather than ceding ground.

Diplomatically, momentum is limited. Western capitals reiterate support for Ukraine while pressing for restraint to reduce civilian harm, and Russia signals it will match any escalation. Without a credible framework for talks, the prospects for a durable pause in hostilities remain remote.

stories.title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer' as ministers face industrial test
stories.summary: Calls to bring British Steel into public ownership by the summer have sharpened scrutiny of the government’s industrial strategy. At the same time, ministers appear relaxed about a wave of Chinese car imports, and Reform UK defends its leader over a £91,000 tax row.
body: Mounting pressure over the future of British Steel has triggered fresh debate in Westminster, with industry voices warning the company may need to be nationalised by the summer to safeguard jobs and critical supply chains. The prospect of state intervention revives long‑running questions over the UK’s capacity to anchor strategic manufacturing.

The government’s wider posture on industry is also in focus after analysis suggested ministers are relatively relaxed about the influx of competitively priced Chinese electric vehicles. Supporters argue lower‑cost imports benefit consumers and accelerate decarbonisation, while critics fear a hollowing‑out of domestic production without stronger safeguards and investment.

Political cross‑currents were further stirred as Reform UK insisted a £91,000 tax issue linked to its leader, Richard Tice, was a minor administrative error. The party’s defence underlines the sensitivity of fiscal probity as economic stewardship and the cost of living dominate the pre‑election conversation.

Together, the steel saga, trade exposure in autos and questions over tax conduct highlight the difficult balance ministers must strike between openness and resilience. Decisions in the coming months will set the tone for the UK’s industrial direction, public finances and credibility with investors and voters alike.

stories.title: 'Every drop of water counts': Fear for the future of Argentina's glaciers
stories.summary: Argentina’s glaciers, vital to water security and ecosystems across the Andes, face mounting pressure from a warming climate. Scientists and local communities warn that losses could imperil drinking water, agriculture, tourism and hydropower.
body: Glaciers in the Andes act as natural reservoirs, releasing meltwater that underpins river systems and sustains communities during dry months. As temperatures rise and precipitation patterns shift, researchers warn that these ice stores are retreating, sharpening worries that every drop of water now counts.

The implications are broad. Reduced glacial mass threatens the reliability of supplies for households and farms, increases stress on urban networks during droughts, and complicates flood management when extreme weather hits. Hydropower, a key component of Argentina’s energy mix, also becomes more vulnerable as seasonal flows grow less predictable.

Tourism and local livelihoods add to the stakes. Iconic landscapes draw visitors from around the world, yet shrinking ice and safety concerns can dampen demand and disrupt jobs. Conservationists caution that short‑term development pressures risk undermining long‑term environmental and economic resilience.

Policymakers face hard choices over funding for monitoring, adaptation and protection, alongside broader efforts to cut emissions. International cooperation and access to climate finance will be critical, but so too will local measures to manage water more efficiently and protect high‑mountain ecosystems.
